\section{Introduction}

The Princeton Laptop Orchestra was not a band with laptops. It was a proposition: that the laptop could be an orchestral instrument, that software could produce spatially rich sound, that a network of computers could constitute an ensemble as legitimate as a string quartet. Dan Trueman, Perry Cook, Scott Smallwood, and Ge Wang built PLOrk in 2005 at Princeton University~\citep{trueman2006plork, trueman2007why}, and the proposition held. The music was real. The performances were compelling. The pedagogy was transformative.

But PLOrk was built at Princeton. Each station required a MacBook (\$1{,}300+), a custom hemispherical speaker~\citep{snyder2015hemispherical}, an 8-channel amplifier, a power conditioner, a rack enclosure, and controllers. Total cost per seat: conservatively \$1{,}500.

This was not a barrier for Princeton. It was a barrier for everyone else.

Over the following decade, laptop orchestras proliferated across universities---SLOrk at Stanford~\citep{wang2009designing}, L2Ork at Virginia Tech~\citep{bukvic2010l2ork}, CLOrk at Concordia, BLOrk at Colorado, and more. At peak, perhaps thirty ensembles existed worldwide. Every single one lived inside a university.

The laptop orchestra movement peaked at approximately 500--1{,}000 participants per year, globally. For comparison, 1.2 million American children play in school bands. The laptop orchestra was a beautiful idea that reached almost no one.

This paper argues that the limiting factor was never artistic---but economic and infrastructural. We present \acos{}, a bare-metal operating system that transforms any surplus x86\_64 laptop into a complete musical instrument at a cost two orders of magnitude below the PLOrk model.

\section{The Laptop Orchestra Landscape}

\subsection{PLOrk: The Gold Standard}

Trueman~\citep{trueman2007why} articulated the core problem: a laptop's built-in speakers produce thin, directionless sound that cannot ``fill a room the way an acoustic instrument does.'' The hemispherical speaker---a geodesic shell with six drivers---gave each station a physical acoustic presence, a \emph{body} for its sound.

This was musically correct and economically disastrous. PLOrk's 15-station setup represented an investment north of \$30{,}000 in equipment alone.

\subsection{L2Ork: The Linux Counter-Proposal}

Ivica Ico Bukvic at Virginia Tech built L2Ork~\citep{bukvic2010l2ork} as an explicit cost reduction: Linux netbooks, Pd-L2Ork, cheaper hemispherical speakers. Result: \$750 per seat, a 50\% reduction.

L2Ork democratized the laptop orchestra within the university system. It did not escape it.

\subsection{The University Trap}

The pattern is consistent across all thirty-plus laptop orchestras:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Enrollment-gated}: only registered students participate
  \item \textbf{Department-funded}: equipment budgets from music or CS departments
  \item \textbf{Space-dependent}: dedicated rehearsal rooms with network infrastructure
  \item \textbf{Staff-dependent}: faculty directors, technical assistants, IT support
  \item \textbf{Semester-bound}: ensembles form and dissolve on academic calendars
  \item \textbf{Geographically fixed}: you must be physically at the university
\end{itemize}

These are structural consequences of the cost model. When each seat costs \$750--1{,}500 and requires custom hardware, the ensemble \emph{must} be institutionally managed.

\subsection{What the Numbers Say}

\begin{table}[h]
\small
\centering
\begin{tabular}{lrrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Model} & \textbf{Seat} & \textbf{30} & \textbf{1000} \\
\midrule
PLOrk       & \$1{,}500+ & \$45K+ & impossible \\
L2Ork       & \$750      & \$22.5K & impossible \\
SLOrk       & \$1{,}200+ & \$36K+ & impossible \\
Sonic Pi    & \$150--200 & \$4.5K & \$150K \\
\textbf{AC OS} & \textbf{\$50--80} & \textbf{\$1.5K} & \textbf{\$50K} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Cost comparison across laptop orchestra models.}
\end{table}

At PLOrk prices, a 1{,}000-person laptop orchestra is a fantasy. At AC~OS prices, it costs less than a single PLOrk setup. The difference is not incremental. It is a phase change.

\section{The Surplus Tsunami}

\subsection{240 Million Machines}

On October 14, 2025, Microsoft ended support for Windows~10~\citep{canalys2024windows}. Canalys estimated that 240 million PCs---machines whose hardware is fully functional but whose processors do not meet Windows~11's requirements---were rendered ``obsolete'' overnight.

These are not ancient machines. They are 2017--2020 era laptops with quad-core processors, 8\,GB RAM, 1080p screens, and WiFi. They became e-waste not because they broke, but because Microsoft decided they were done.

\subsection{The Global E-Waste Context}

The UN Global E-waste Monitor 2024~\citep{forti2024ewaste} reports 62 million tonnes of e-waste generated globally per year, growing at 2.6 million tonnes annually. Only 22.3\% is documented as properly collected and recycled.

Every surplus laptop diverted from this stream into a musical instrument is a small environmental intervention. At scale, a program that repurposes even 0.1\% of the annual PC surplus would produce 240{,}000 instruments per year.

\subsection{The Chromebook Graveyard}

The U.S. PIRG report~\citep{pirg2023chromebook} documents how school districts cycle through Chromebooks every 3--5 years, spending \$1.8 billion on devices designed to be replaced. A district that flashed retired Chromebooks with \acos{} could turn its e-waste budget into an instrument program.

\subsection{Surplus Pricing}

\begin{table}[h]
\small
\centering
\begin{tabular}{lr}
\toprule
\textbf{Machine} & \textbf{Price} \\
\midrule
ThinkPad X1 Carbon (6--8th) & \$40--80 \\
ThinkPad T480/T490 & \$35--70 \\
HP EliteBook 840 G5/G6 & \$40--75 \\
Dell Latitude 7400/7410 & \$45--80 \\
Lenovo IdeaPad (various) & \$25--50 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Surplus laptop pricing, March 2026.}
\end{table}

Every one boots \acos{} in under 8 seconds. Every one has a keyboard, trackpad, mic, speakers, screen, and WiFi. Every one is a complete musical instrument waiting for an 89\,MB file.

\section{AC Native OS as Instrument}

Where PLOrk required assembling an instrument from components, \acos{} proposes that the surplus laptop \emph{already is} the instrument. It merely needs to be told.

\subsection{Architecture in Brief}

\acos{} is a single 89\,MB UEFI binary containing a Linux kernel, an LZ4-compressed initramfs, and an 11{,}943-line C runtime that runs as PID~1~\citep{scudder2026os}. There is no userspace, no desktop, no package manager, no shell.

Boot time: \textbf{7.3 seconds} from power button to playable instrument.

\subsection{Built-In Synthesis}

The runtime includes a 32-voice polyphonic synthesizer with ADSR envelopes, multiple waveforms, and a global reverb processor. Audio runs at 192\,kHz through ALSA PCM.

This is the critical divergence from PLOrk. Trueman's hemispherical speaker solved spatial sound projection. \acos{} sidesteps the problem entirely: the built-in laptop speakers \emph{are} the speakers. Not omnidirectional, not hemispherical, not acoustically ideal. But \emph{there}.

For a 15-person PLOrk in a concert hall, hemispherical speakers are the right answer. For a 1{,}000-person distributed ensemble in classrooms, parks, and living rooms, the laptop's own speakers are the right answer.

\subsection{The Laptop Already Has Everything}

\begin{table}[h]
\small
\centering
\begin{tabular}{ll}
\toprule
\textbf{PLOrk Requires} & \textbf{Surplus Has} \\
\midrule
MacBook & Any x86\_64 laptop \\
Hemi speaker & Built-in speakers \\
8-ch amplifier & Built-in amp \\
Power conditioner & AC adapter / battery \\
Rack enclosure & Laptop chassis \\
MIDI controller & Keyboard + trackpad \\
ChucK/Max license & AC OS (free) \\
Network infra & WiFi (built-in) \\
IT staff & OTA updates (auto) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{PLOrk requirements vs. surplus laptop capabilities.}
\end{table}

\subsection{Network-Native Ensemble}

\ac{} includes built-in multiplayer networking: WebSocket for reliable events and UDP for low-latency real-time data. Any \acos{} device on the same WiFi can join an ensemble session with zero configuration.

Three people with surplus laptops in a park can form a laptop orchestra.

\subsection{Personalization as Identity}

Each \acos{} device boots with its owner's handle displayed and spoken aloud: ``hi @maria.'' The machine is not a generic terminal---it is \emph{Maria's instrument}, and it knows it.

Every orchestra instrument has a name. Now every laptop does too.

\section{The Dedicated-Device Landscape}

\subsection{Nintendo Switch (\$299)}

The Switch is a handheld computer with touchscreen, speakers, WiFi, motion sensors, and detachable controllers. It runs a custom OS that boots in seconds. Users navigate programs by name. No file manager, no desktop.

The structural similarity to \acos{} is striking. The Switch has sold 146 million units~\citep{nintendo2024earnings}---proof that people adopt dedicated devices when the experience is coherent and the barrier is a power button.

The difference: the Switch costs \$299. A surplus ThinkPad running \acos{} costs \$50.

\subsection{Steam Deck (\$399)}

Valve's Steam Deck is a handheld x86\_64 PC running custom Linux that boots directly into a game library---architecturally almost exactly what \acos{} does. Valve has sold 5--10 million units. The Steam Deck proves the model. \acos{} runs it on hardware that already exists.

\subsection{Teenage Engineering}

The OP-1 Field (\$1{,}999), EP-133 (\$299), TX-6 (\$1{,}199). Purpose-built devices for music creation. Beautiful objects. Functionally, luxury goods. At \$2{,}000, inaccessible to those who most need creative instruments.

An OP-1 for every child in a school costs \$600{,}000 for 300 units. A surplus laptop for every child costs \$15{,}000. The question is not whether a ThinkPad is as beautiful as an OP-1. It is whether beauty should be a prerequisite for access.

\subsection{The Insight}

The consumer market has already answered ``Will people use dedicated devices?'' with 146 million Switches and 5--10 million Steam Decks. \acos{} applies the model to surplus hardware: same pattern, radically different economics.

The surplus laptop is not a degraded general-purpose computer. It is an \emph{unrealized dedicated device}---one that was always capable of being an instrument, but was running Windows instead.

\section{From Ensemble to Planet}

\subsection{PLOrk's Scaling Limit}

PLOrk serves 15 people per semester. Over twenty years: approximately 600 individual participants. To scale to 1{,}000 seats is physically impossible---the hemispherical speakers alone would require a warehouse.

The PLOrk model scales \emph{linearly with cost and logarithmically with complexity}. It is beautiful, but it cannot reproduce.

\subsection{AC OS's Scaling Curve}

\acos{} scales linearly with cost and \emph{sublinearly with complexity}. Each additional instrument: one surplus laptop, one USB flash, sixty seconds. The OTA update system means every instrument maintains itself.

\begin{table}[h]
\small
\centering
\begin{tabular}{lrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Scale} & \textbf{PLOrk} & \textbf{AC OS} \\
\midrule
15 seats & \$22{,}500 & \$750 \\
100 seats & \$150{,}000 & \$5{,}000 \\
1{,}000 seats & impossible & \$50{,}000 \\
10{,}000 seats & impossible & \$500K \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Scaling costs. PLOrk becomes impossible beyond $\sim$50 seats.}
\end{table}

\subsection{The 240-Million-Seat Orchestra}

If 1\% of Windows~10 surplus machines were flashed with \acos{}, the result: 2.4 million creative instruments. OLPC deployed 3 million XO laptops over its entire lifetime---at a cost of billions. One percent of the surplus, at zero manufacturing cost, matches OLPC's entire deployment.

The instruments already exist. They are sitting in closets, warehouses, and recycling bins. The software to activate them is 89\,MB.

\section{PLOrk the Planet: A Proposal}

\subsection{Community Flashing Events}

A community event where participants bring surplus laptops and leave with instruments. One person with USB drives and a table. Each machine is personalized (``What's your name?''), flashed (sixty seconds), booted (seven seconds), and handed back.

Simpler than a voter registration drive, cheaper than a school bake sale.

\subsection{School Programs Without Budgets}

A school district retiring Windows~10 machines can redirect the hardware to an instrument program instead of a recycler. The ``equipment budget'' is zero---the equipment already exists. Surplus ThinkPads from corporate donors at \$30--50 each produce a 30-instrument classroom ensemble for \$900--1{,}500.

\subsection{Beyond the University}

At \$50 per instrument with zero ongoing costs, laptop ensembles become viable for:

\begin{itemize}
  \item After-school programs and community centers
  \item Youth orchestras and maker spaces
  \item Senior centers and retirement communities
  \item Incarcerated populations (no internet required)
  \item Refugee camps (battery-powered, WiFi optional)
  \item Rural and remote communities
\end{itemize}

None of these contexts could support a PLOrk station. All can support a surplus laptop.

\subsection{The Distributed Orchestra}

Because \acos{} networking operates over standard WiFi and internet, a laptop orchestra need not be in the same room: city-wide simultaneous performances, school-to-school ensembles, cross-border telematic events, asynchronous relay compositions passed between time zones.

\section{What PLOrk Got Right}

This paper is not a critique of PLOrk. PLOrk is one of the most important developments in computer music:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Legitimacy}: proved laptop ensembles produce real music
  \item \textbf{Spatialization}: demonstrated that electronic instruments benefit from acoustic presence
  \item \textbf{Composition}: generated new repertoire~\citep{smallwood2008composing} and strategies for networked instruments
  \item \textbf{Pedagogy}: a model for creative computing education~\citep{trueman2007why}
  \item \textbf{Community}: created a new social form
\end{enumerate}

What PLOrk missed---and what was not its mission to solve---was access. The proposition here is not to replace PLOrk, but to fulfill the dream it started: that a network of computers could be a new kind of orchestra. PLOrk proved it could be. \acos{} proposes to make it so cheap that the proof becomes planetary.

\section{Music, Access, and Political Economy}

Attali argued in \emph{Noise}~\citep{attali1985noise} that music is prophetic: changes in music anticipate changes in political economy. The laptop orchestra, in Attali's framework, is a composition-era ensemble---but the university laptop orchestra gates participation behind enrollment and tuition. It is composition-era music with representation-era access controls.

Turino~\citep{turino2008music} distinguishes \emph{participatory} music (success = degree of participation) from \emph{presentational} music (success = quality for an audience). A planetary laptop orchestra is participatory by default. There is no stage. There are only participants.

Illich's ``tools for conviviality''~\citep{illich1973tools} completes the frame. A convivial tool can be used by anyone, without specialized training, to accomplish purposes they determine themselves. A PLOrk station is not a convivial tool. A surplus laptop running \acos{} approaches conviviality: it boots into a playable instrument, requires no configuration, and belongs to its owner.

\section{Lessons from OLPC}

OLPC~\citep{negroponte1995being, kraemer2009olpc} deployed 3 million XO laptops at a cost of billions. It failed because: purpose-built unfamiliar hardware, proprietary parts, no software ecosystem, government coordination requirements, and high per-unit cost.

\acos{} inverts every failure mode:

\begin{table}[h]
\small
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\linewidth}{lXX}
\toprule
\textbf{Failure} & \textbf{OLPC} & \textbf{AC OS} \\
\midrule
Hardware & XO (\$188) & Surplus (\$50) \\
Form & Unfamiliar & Standard laptop \\
Repair & Proprietary & Commodity \\
Deploy & Govt. ministry & One person + USB \\
Scale & 3M over 10yr & 240M already exist \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\caption{OLPC failure modes vs. AC OS.}
\end{table}

The deepest lesson: hardware-first deployments fail when they require manufacturing, logistics, and institutional coordination. \acos{} requires none of these because the hardware already exists. The deployment mechanism is a file, not a supply chain.

\section{Limitations}

\textbf{Audio quality.} Built-in laptop speakers are acoustically poor. For ensembles larger than a room, external amplification is needed.

\textbf{ARM support.} \acos{} targets x86\_64 only. Most educational Chromebooks use ARM processors. This is the most critical engineering gap.

\textbf{No formal evaluation.} No user study, no controlled experiment. The access claims are grounded in hardware economics, not empirical pedagogy.

\textbf{Single-author project.} The bus factor is one. A planetary deployment cannot depend on a single maintainer.

\textbf{Network latency.} Internet latency (50--200ms) fundamentally changes what music is possible. ``Latency as compositional parameter'' is an open research problem, not a solved one.

\textbf{No curriculum.} The instrument exists; the pedagogy does not. Without it, deploying hardware into schools risks repeating OLPC's pattern.

\section{Future Work}

\textbf{ARM Chromebook support}---the most impactful engineering target, unlocking millions of additional instruments from school surplus.

\textbf{Built-in composition tools}---sequencers, pattern editors, looping tools, and collaborative composition interfaces as pieces.

\textbf{Spatialization without speakers}---using the ensemble's distributed physical positions to create emergent spatial audio fields. A ring of 30 laptops \emph{is} a hemispherical speaker, if the software knows where each one is.

\textbf{Curriculum development}---adapting PLOrk's pedagogy~\citep{trueman2007why} for zero-infrastructure contexts.

\textbf{Mesh networking}---peer-to-peer ensemble connectivity without internet infrastructure for outdoor spaces, rural areas, and developing regions.

\section{Conclusion}

In 2005, Dan Trueman asked ``Why a laptop orchestra?''~\citep{trueman2007why} and gave a compelling answer. He was right. PLOrk proved it.

Twenty years later, the question is different. Not ``why a laptop orchestra?'' but ``why only \emph{one}?''

The answer has been economic. The laptop orchestra was expensive, so it stayed in the university.

\acos{} removes the economic barrier. An 89\,MB file. A \$50 surplus laptop. Seven seconds to boot. Built-in synthesis, networking, personalization, updates. No speakers to build, no software to install, no IT department, no institution.

With 240 million PCs entering surplus from Windows~10 alone, and 62 million tonnes of e-waste annually~\citep{forti2024ewaste}, the raw material for a planetary laptop orchestra is embarrassingly abundant. The hardware exists. The software exists. The network exists. The music, as PLOrk showed us twenty years ago, is real.

PLOrk'ing the planet is not a metaphor. It is a logistics problem. And the logistics just got a hundred times cheaper.
